---
title: "04 · Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors"
subtitle: "Special directions a matrix only stretches"
lesson-id: "t5.eigenvalues"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t5.linear-systems-gaussian", "t5.vector-spaces"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [02 · Linear Systems](../02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)
   and [03 · Vector Spaces](../03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with matrix-vector multiplication and determinants?
3. Recall solving polynomial equations (Tier 3)?
:::

## Touch

Pick a matrix. The widget points out the directions that the matrix
*only stretches* — never rotates — and reports the stretch factor for
each.

```{ojs}
viewof eigSel = Inputs.select(["A=[2,0;0,3]: eigenvectors are axes", "A=[3,1;0,2]: upper triangular", "A=[0,-1;1,0]: rotation by 90° (no real eigenvectors)"], {value: "A=[2,0;0,3]: eigenvectors are axes", label: "Matrix:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(eigSel) {
    case "A=[2,0;0,3]: eigenvectors are axes": info = "Eigenvalues: 2, 3. Eigenvectors: e₁=(1,0) (scaled by 2), e₂=(0,1) (scaled by 3). Diagonal matrix's eigenvectors are the standard basis."; break;
    case "A=[3,1;0,2]: upper triangular": info = "Eigenvalues: 3, 2 (diagonal entries of triangular matrix). Eigenvectors: (1,0) for λ=3; (1,−1) for λ=2."; break;
    case "A=[0,-1;1,0]: rotation by 90° (no real eigenvectors)": info = "Eigenvalues: ±i (complex). No real direction is preserved — every vector rotates 90°."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0; text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1rem; color:#c2185b;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why might "directions a matrix only stretches" be useful?
2. Why does a rotation matrix have no real eigenvectors?
3. The product of eigenvalues = the determinant. Why?

## Discover

### Problem 1

For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, verify $(1,
0)$ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue 2.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A (1, 0)^T = (2, 0)^T = 2 \cdot (1, 0)^T$ ✓.

Diagonal matrices have the standard basis vectors as eigenvectors,
with the diagonal entries as eigenvalues.
</details>

### Problem 2

For general $A$, the equation $A v = \lambda v$ rewrites as $(A -
\lambda I) v = 0$. For non-zero $v$, the matrix $A - \lambda I$ must
be **singular**, i.e., $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. This is the
**characteristic equation**.

For $A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$, compute the
characteristic polynomial.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 4 - \lambda & 1 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda
\end{pmatrix}$. Determinant.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det = (4 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - 2 = \lambda^2 - 7 \lambda + 10$.

Set to zero: $(\lambda - 5)(\lambda - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 5,
2$. **Eigenvalues 5 and 2.**

To find eigenvectors: solve $(A - \lambda I) v = 0$ for each $\lambda$.
For $\lambda = 5$: $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} v
= 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2$. Eigenvector: $(1, 1)^T$ (or any scalar
multiple).
</details>

### Problem 3

Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1
\\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ (90° rotation).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det(A - \lambda I) = \lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \pm i$.
**No real eigenvalues.**

Geometrically: a 90° rotation has *no* direction preserved (every
vector rotates). Real eigenvalues correspond to "preserved
directions" — rotations have none unless trivial. (Complex
eigenvalues describe rotations + scalings in the *complex* sense.)
</details>

### Problem 4

Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1
& 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Char eq: $(1 - \lambda)^2 - 1 = \lambda^2 - 2\lambda = \lambda(\lambda
- 2) = 0$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Eigenvalues: $\lambda = 0, 2$.

For $\lambda = 0$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} v =
0 \Rightarrow v_1 + v_2 = 0$. Eigenvector: $(1, -1)^T$.

For $\lambda = 2$: $\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} v
= 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2$. Eigenvector: $(1, 1)^T$.

A zero eigenvalue means $A$ has a *null space* — there's a non-zero
vector mapped to zero. The matrix is *singular*.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Diagonalisation.** If a matrix $A$ has $n$ independent
eigenvectors, $A = P D P^{-1}$ where $P$ is the matrix of
eigenvectors and $D$ is diagonal with eigenvalues. Then $A^k = P D^k
P^{-1}$ — easy power computation.

What's $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}^{10}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
From P4: eigenvalues 0, 2. $D = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2
\end{pmatrix}$.
$D^{10} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1024 \end{pmatrix}$.

$P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, P^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{2}
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

$A^{10} = P D^{10} P^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 512 & 512 \\ 512 & 512
\end{pmatrix}$.

(Check: each step doubles the matrix entries since $A^2 = 2A$, so
$A^{10} = 2^9 A = 512 A$ ✓.)

**Diagonalisation makes powers easy** — a major reason eigenvalues
matter.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For a square matrix $A$, a non-zero vector $v$ is an **eigenvector**
with **eigenvalue** $\lambda$ if $A v = \lambda v$.

**Characteristic equation:** $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. Roots are the
eigenvalues.

**Eigenvectors** for each $\lambda$ are non-zero vectors in the **null
space** of $(A - \lambda I)$.

**Properties:**

- Sum of eigenvalues = $\text{trace}(A)$ (sum of diagonal).
- Product of eigenvalues = $\det(A)$.
- Diagonal/triangular matrices have eigenvalues = diagonal entries.

**Diagonalisation:** if $A$ has $n$ linearly independent
eigenvectors, $A = P D P^{-1}$. Then $A^k = P D^k P^{-1}$.

Real symmetric matrices are *always* diagonalisable, with real
eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. (Spectral theorem.)

:::

> *Eigenvectors: directions a matrix only stretches;*
> *Eigenvalues: the stretching factors;*
> *Diagonalisation = "find eigenvectors as a basis" — hugely useful for powers.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\
> 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Triangular → eigenvalues are diagonal entries: **3, 2**.

For $\lambda = 3$: $(A - 3I) v = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1
\end{pmatrix} v = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = 0$. Eigenvector: $(1, 0)^T$.

For $\lambda = 2$: $(A - 2I) v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0
\end{pmatrix} v = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 + v_2 = 0$. Eigenvector: $(1,
-1)^T$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Eigenvalues of $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5, 7$.
</details>

**W2.** Trace of $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 + 2 = 5$.
</details>

**W3.** $\det \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6$, also = product of eigenvalues.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Eigenvalues of $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 10 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 5, 2$. (Discover
P2.)
</details>

**P2.** Eigenvectors for $\lambda = 5$ in P1?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(A - 5I) v = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2$. $(1, 1)^T$.
</details>

**P3.** Compute $\det A$ via product of eigenvalues for the matrix
in P1.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 \cdot 2 = 10$. Direct: $4 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 2 = 10$ ✓.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **PCA in ML.** **Principal Component Analysis** decomposes a
data covariance matrix into eigenvectors (the *principal components*).
Why are the eigenvectors with the largest eigenvalues the "most
informative" directions?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Eigenvalues of the covariance matrix = variances along the
corresponding eigenvector directions.** The eigenvector with largest
eigenvalue is the direction of *greatest variance* — where the data
spreads most. PCA projects high-dim data onto top-$k$ eigenvectors,
preserving as much variance as possible while reducing dimension.

PCA is *the* classic dimensionality reduction technique. Modern
self-supervised representation learning (SimCLR, BYOL) often
*implicitly* learns directions analogous to PCA components.
</details>

**S2.** **Google's PageRank** algorithm assigns each web page an
*importance score* given by the eigenvector of the link matrix
corresponding to eigenvalue 1. Why does this work?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The link matrix $A_{ij}$ encodes "fraction of page $j$'s outgoing
links that go to page $i$." The eigenvector $r$ with $A r = r$
satisfies "score $r_i$ equals weighted sum of scores of pages linking
*to* $i$" — exactly the recursive definition of importance.

Existence is guaranteed by the **Perron-Frobenius theorem** for non-
negative matrices. The eigenvector is computed iteratively (the
*power method*).

Google's $250B+ valuation initially rested on this eigenvalue
calculation. Linear algebra you learned in 30 minutes built one of
the most consequential companies of the modern era.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Every matrix has real eigenvalues."** No — rotations have only
   complex (non-real) eigenvalues.
2. **"Eigenvectors are unique."** Eigenvectors are unique up to
   *scalar multiple*. The whole *eigenspace* (1D for distinct
   eigenvalues, possibly higher for repeated) shares the same
   eigenvalue.
3. **"All matrices diagonalise."** No — *defective* matrices have
   fewer eigenvectors than dimensions; they don't diagonalise. (Jordan
   form handles them, Tier 6+.)
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you compute the characteristic polynomial of a 2×2 matrix?
2. Without scrolling back, what are the eigenvalues of $\begin{pmatrix}
   6 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $6, -2$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[05 · Partial Derivatives](../05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (PageRank).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[02 · Linear Systems](../02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)** —
  to find eigenvectors.
- **[03 · Vector Spaces](../03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)** —
  eigenspaces are subspaces.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 6 — Spectral Theorem** — symmetric matrices fully
  diagonalise.
- **Tier 7 — Functional Analysis** — operators on infinite-dimensional
  spaces (eigenvalues become spectra).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** PCA (S1), spectral methods, attention
in Transformers (computing eigenvalues for matrix decompositions),
random matrix theory in deep learning theory.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Search & ranking.** PageRank (S2), HITS, modern recommender
systems all use eigenvector computations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics.** Quantum mechanics: observables are operators;
eigenvalues are *measured values*; eigenvectors are *energy
states*. Atomic and molecular structure is fundamentally an
eigenvalue problem.
:::
